findings.title: Package @proj-airi/desktop-shell has no consumer in monorepo
findings.code_snippet: "name": "@proj-airi/desktop-shell"
findings.description: The new @proj-airi/desktop-shell package exports getActiveWindow, ClipboardMonitor, and ShortcutManager, but no other package or app in the monorepo imports or depends on it. A grep for '@proj-airi/desktop-shell' and 'desktop-shell' across all .ts, .json, and .vue files returns only self-references (its own package.json, vitest.config.ts entry, and tasks.json). This is orphan code -- dead-on-arrival with no live entry point in any application.
findings.suggestion: Wire the package into at least one consumer (e.g., apps/stage-tamagotchi Electron main process services) before committing. Add it as a dependency in the consumer's package.json and import/instantiate its exports from a service that runs at app startup. The integration-rules require every new module to trace to at least one live entry point.

findings.title: No structured logging in desktop-shell package
findings.code_snippet: reject(new Error('Failed to get active window', { cause: error }))
findings.description: The entire desktop-shell package (active-window.ts, clipboard-monitor.ts, global-shortcuts.ts) contains zero logging. Errors in getActiveWindow are rejected but never logged. ClipboardMonitor and ShortcutManager delegate errors to an optional onError callback but produce no structured log output. The project standard requires @guiiai/logg (useLogg pattern) for all new packages. Without logging, failures in window detection, clipboard polling, or shortcut registration will be invisible in production.
findings.suggestion: Add @guiiai/logg as a dependency and create a logger via useLogg('desktop-shell') or per-module loggers (useLogg('desktop-shell:active-window'), etc.). Log at INFO level on start/stop lifecycle events, WARN on recoverable errors (shortcut registration failure, clipboard read failure), and ERROR on fatal failures (getActiveWindow rejection).

findings.title: ShortcutManager.registered set true even when registrations fail
findings.description: After iterating over all bindings, line 49 unconditionally sets this.registered = true regardless of how many registrations actually succeeded. If all registrations fail (returning false or throwing), the manager still reports isRegistered === true and subsequent registerAll() calls become no-ops. This means a consumer cannot retry registration after transient failures. Additionally, unregisterAll() will call the underlying unregisterAllFn even though nothing was successfully registered.
findings.suggestion: Track whether at least one binding registered successfully. Either: (a) set this.registered = true only if successCount > 0, or (b) track per-binding registration state so unregisterAll can skip unneeded cleanup and registerAll can retry failed bindings. A simple fix: add a successCount variable, increment on success, and set this.registered = successCount > 0.

findings.title: throw inside setInterval callback causes uncaught exception
findings.description: When onError is not provided, the catch block re-throws inside a setInterval callback (line 27: `setInterval(() => this.poll(), ...)`). Throwing from a timer callback results in an uncaught exception that will crash the Node.js process or be silently swallowed depending on the environment. Unlike synchronous callers, there is no call stack to catch this throw. This is a process-killing bug in production.
findings.suggestion: Never re-throw from a timer callback. Either require onError in the constructor (make it non-optional), or add a fallback that logs via console.error and stops the monitor: `else { console.error(error); this.stop(); }`. The type definition in types.ts should also be updated to make onError required if the re-throw path is removed.

findings.title: start() lacks try/catch on initial readClipboard() call
findings.code_snippet: this.lastText = this.readClipboard()
findings.description: The start() method calls this.readClipboard() without any error handling. If the clipboard read fails (e.g., permissions denied, clipboard unavailable), a raw error propagates to the caller with no context about what operation failed. The poll() method properly wraps readClipboard errors, but start() does not. This inconsistency means the first read can crash while subsequent reads are handled.
findings.suggestion: Wrap the initial readClipboard() call in try/catch with the same error handling pattern used in poll(): `try { this.lastText = this.readClipboard() } catch (err) { const error = new Error('ClipboardMonitor failed to read initial clipboard state', { cause: err }); if (this.onError) { this.onError(error) } else { throw error } }`

findings.title: onChange callback errors caught by poll() catch-all
findings.description: The onChange callback is called inside the try block of poll(). If onChange throws, the error is caught by the catch block and wrapped as 'ClipboardMonitor poll failed', which misattributes the error. The caller's callback error is treated as a clipboard read failure. This is the same callback-error-swallowing pattern seen in ScreenshotPipeline and ActivityMonitor in this codebase.
findings.suggestion: Move the onChange call outside the try block, or wrap it in its own try/catch with a distinct error message: `try { this.onChange({...}) } catch (cbErr) { this.reportError(new Error('ClipboardMonitor onChange callback failed', { cause: cbErr })) }`

findings.title: onAction callback in shortcut handler has no error handling
findings.description: The callback passed to registerFn (which runs when a global shortcut is pressed) calls this.onAction without any try/catch. This callback executes asynchronously when the user presses the shortcut -- if onAction throws, the error propagates uncaught from the Electron global shortcut handler. This is the same pattern as the setInterval throw in ClipboardMonitor: no caller exists to catch the error.
findings.suggestion: Wrap the onAction call in try/catch: `() => { try { this.onAction(binding.action) } catch (err) { this.reportError(new Error(`Shortcut action '${binding.action}' failed`, { cause: err })) } }`

findings.title: unregisterAllFn() called without try/catch -- inconsistent state
findings.code_snippet:
this.unregisterAllFn()
this.registered = false
findings.description: If unregisterAllFn() throws, the registered flag remains true, leaving the ShortcutManager in an inconsistent state where it believes shortcuts are registered but unregister failed. The next call to registerAll() would return early (line 28: `if (this.registered) return`) and the system would be stuck with no working shortcuts and no way to re-register.
findings.suggestion: Wrap in try/finally: `try { this.unregisterAllFn() } finally { this.registered = false }`. This ensures registered is reset even on failure. Also consider reporting the error via reportError.

findings.title: ActiveWindowInfo uses pid 0 as sentinel, conflicting with real PID 0
findings.description: Encapsulation: 7/10, Expression: 4/10, Usefulness: 5/10, Enforcement: 3/10 (Aggregate: 4.75). parseAppleScriptOutput returns { appName: '', windowTitle: '', pid: 0 } when there is no output, and also returns pid 0 when parsing fails (line 34). PID 0 is a valid Unix process (kernel/init). Callers cannot distinguish 'no active window detected' from 'the kernel process is frontmost'. The empty-string + zero sentinel pattern makes illegal states representable: a return value that looks valid but means 'no data'. This violates Make Illegal States Unrepresentable.
findings.suggestion: Return null from parseAppleScriptOutput when no data is available, or use a discriminated union result type: { ok: true, info: ActiveWindowInfo } | { ok: false, reason: string }. Alternatively, make pid optional (pid?: number) so callers must handle the absence case explicitly.

findings.title: ShortcutManager marks registered=true even when all registrations fail
findings.code_snippet: this.registered = true
findings.description: When an onError handler is provided, registerAll() swallows all registration failures and unconditionally sets this.registered = true on line 49. This means isRegistered returns true even when zero shortcuts were successfully registered. The test at global-shortcuts.test.ts:62-78 ('reports registration failure via onError') verifies errors are emitted but never asserts manager.isRegistered afterward -- missing the bug. This is both a logic bug in the implementation and a gap in test coverage.
findings.suggestion: Add a test case: after registerAll() where every binding fails (register returns false) with onError set, assert that manager.isRegistered is false (or that a partial-success state is tracked). Fix the implementation to only set registered = true if at least one binding succeeded, or track per-binding registration status.

findings.title: ClipboardMonitor.start() unhandled throw when readClipboard fails on init
findings.code_snippet: this.lastText = this.readClipboard()
findings.description: ClipboardMonitor.start() calls this.readClipboard() directly at line 26 without try/catch. If readClipboard throws during the initial read, the error propagates uncaught -- the onError handler is only used inside poll(). The timer is never set, leaving the monitor in a partially initialized state. No test covers this scenario. This is an error path that callers have no way to handle gracefully through the onError callback.
findings.suggestion: Add a test: construct ClipboardMonitor with readClipboard that throws on first call, call start(), verify it either (a) routes to onError and does not start polling, or (b) propagates the error. Then fix start() to wrap the initial readClipboard() call in try/catch consistent with poll() behavior.

findings.title: getActiveWindow hardcodes macOS AppleScript with no platform guard
findings.code_snippet:
const APPLESCRIPT = `
tell application "System Events"
  set frontApp to first application process whose frontmost is true
  ...
end tell
`
findings.description: The getActiveWindow function uses osascript (AppleScript) which only works on macOS. The function has no runtime platform check and will fail with a cryptic error on Windows/Linux. The package description says 'macOS desktop integration' but the exported API gives no indication of platform restriction. The integration test guards with describe.runIf(platform() === 'darwin') but the production code has no such guard. The package lists std-env as a dependency (presumably for platform detection) but never imports it.
findings.suggestion: Add an explicit platform check at the top of getActiveWindow() that rejects with a clear error message on non-macOS platforms: if (process.platform !== 'darwin') reject(new Error('getActiveWindow is only supported on macOS')). Alternatively, if std-env was intended for this purpose, import and use its isMacOS check. Either way, document the platform restriction in the JSDoc and README.

findings.title: std-env declared as dependency but never imported
findings.description: The package.json declares std-env as a runtime dependency, but no source file under packages/desktop-shell/src/ imports from 'std-env'. A grep across the entire src directory confirms zero references. This is dead weight in the dependency tree and suggests an incomplete implementation where platform detection was planned but not wired up.
findings.suggestion: Either remove std-env from dependencies (if platform detection is not needed), or import and use it for the platform guard in active-window.ts (e.g., import { isMacOS } from 'std-env'). Do not ship unused dependencies.

findings.title: ShortcutManagerOptions.unregister is declared but never used
findings.code_snippet: unregister: (accelerator: string) => void
findings.description: The ShortcutManagerOptions interface requires an 'unregister' function (line 30) for removing individual shortcut registrations. However, ShortcutManager never references or calls this function -- it only stores and calls unregisterAll (the bulk variant). Consumers must provide this function to satisfy the type contract but it is silently ignored. This is a dead interface member that creates confusion about expected behavior.
findings.suggestion: Either (a) remove 'unregister' from ShortcutManagerOptions if per-shortcut unregistration is not needed, or (b) implement per-binding unregistration in ShortcutManager (e.g., for re-registering a single changed binding). Option (a) is simpler and avoids ISP violation.

findings.title: ClipboardMonitor.lastText is ephemeral in-memory state
findings.code_snippet:
private lastText: string
private timer: ReturnType<typeof setInterval> | null = null
...
this.lastText = ''
findings.description: ClipboardMonitor tracks lastText (the previously seen clipboard content) as an in-memory private field initialized to empty string. On service restart, this will be reset and the first poll will trigger a spurious onChange event for whatever is currently in the clipboard (since it will differ from the empty initial value). For a desktop app that restarts, this means a false clipboard-change notification on every launch.
findings.suggestion: Accept an optional initialText parameter in ClipboardMonitorOptions (defaulting to undefined/null) and, when provided, use it to seed lastText. Alternatively, in the start() method, read the clipboard once to initialize lastText before starting the interval timer (which it already does on line 26). The current code at line 26 does handle this correctly for the first start -- but if stop() is called and start() is called again, the stale lastText from the previous run is kept. Consider whether re-start should re-read the clipboard to avoid false positives.

findings.title: DEFAULT_BINDINGS hardcodes shortcut accelerators
findings.code_snippet:
export const DEFAULT_BINDINGS = [
  { accelerator: 'CommandOrControl+Shift+A', action: 'toggle-panel' as ShortcutAction },
  ...
] as const
findings.description: Shortcut key accelerators are hardcoded as compile-time constants. Users who need to customize shortcuts (accessibility, keyboard layout conflicts) have no mechanism to override them without code changes. While ShortcutManager accepts bindings as a constructor option, the DEFAULT_BINDINGS constant encourages consumers to pass it directly, baking in the specific key combinations.
findings.suggestion: Make DEFAULT_BINDINGS a fallback that can be overridden via user configuration. The consumer (e.g., Electron main process) should load shortcut bindings from a persisted configuration store (user preferences) and fall back to DEFAULT_BINDINGS only when no custom config exists. This is a design consideration for the integration layer rather than a change to this package, but document the intent clearly.

findings.title: registerAll() sets registered=true even when some bindings fail
findings.code_snippet: this.registered = true
findings.description: After the for-loop that attempts to register each binding, `this.registered` is set to true unconditionally. If some bindings failed (either via `success === false` or via caught exceptions), the manager still reports itself as fully registered. The caller has no way to know that only some shortcuts are active. With onError provided, registration errors are silently reported but the system proceeds as if everything worked.
findings.suggestion: Track per-binding registration state, or at minimum track whether any binding failed. Consider returning a result object from registerAll(): `{ registered: number, failed: number, errors: Error[] }`. Only set this.registered = true if all bindings succeeded, or add a separate `isPartiallyRegistered` state.

findings.title: parseAppleScriptOutput returns valid-looking empty result on failure
findings.description: When the AppleScript output is empty (which could indicate a failure, no focused window, or an OS-level issue), parseAppleScriptOutput returns `{ appName: '', windowTitle: '', pid: 0 }`. This is indistinguishable from a legitimate result where the active app has no name. The caller cannot differentiate between 'no data available' and 'parse failed'. This follows the same error-as-valid-state pattern seen in ReportGenerator (empty DailySummary) and SmartTodo (empty suggestions) in this codebase.
findings.suggestion: Return `null` for empty/unparseable input so callers can distinguish 'no active window data' from a window with empty fields. Alternatively, use a Result type: `{ ok: true, value: ActiveWindowInfo } | { ok: false, reason: string }`.

findings.title: No JSDoc on exported interfaces/types in types.ts
findings.description: The project convention (confirmed in MEMORY.md and CLAUDE.md) is that all exported interfaces and functions have JSDoc. This file exports 6 public types (ActiveWindowInfo, ClipboardChange, ClipboardMonitorOptions, ShortcutAction, ShortcutBinding, ShortcutManagerOptions) with zero JSDoc. While the type names are reasonably self-documenting, consumers of this package need to understand semantics: e.g., what does pid=0 mean in ActiveWindowInfo? Is timestamp in ClipboardChange epoch-ms or epoch-seconds? What format is the accelerator string in ShortcutBinding?
findings.suggestion: Add JSDoc to each exported interface/type. At minimum document non-obvious fields: ActiveWindowInfo.pid (0 when unavailable), ClipboardChange.timestamp (epoch milliseconds), ShortcutBinding.accelerator (Electron-style accelerator string format).

findings.title: No JSDoc on exported functions in active-window.ts
findings.description: Two exported functions (parseAppleScriptOutput, getActiveWindow) lack JSDoc. parseAppleScriptOutput is exported and available to consumers but its contract is non-obvious: it expects a tab-delimited string from osascript. getActiveWindow has a 5-second timeout and is macOS-only, neither of which is documented. A consumer calling getActiveWindow on Linux would get an opaque 'Failed to get active window' error with no guidance.
findings.suggestion: Add JSDoc to both functions. For getActiveWindow, document: (1) macOS-only platform requirement, (2) the 5000ms timeout, (3) the error thrown on failure. For parseAppleScriptOutput, document the expected input format (tab-delimited: appName\twindowTitle\tpid) and that it returns zeroed fields on empty input.

findings.title: No JSDoc on ClipboardMonitor class and public methods
findings.description: The ClipboardMonitor class is exported with 3 public members (start, stop, isRunning) and no JSDoc on the class or any of them. The polling mechanism, the idempotent start/stop behavior, and the fact that start() synchronously reads the clipboard (and can throw) are all non-obvious details that should be documented for consumers.
findings.suggestion: Add JSDoc to the class describing its purpose (poll-based clipboard change detection) and to each public method. Document that start() is idempotent (no-op if already running), that it immediately reads the clipboard, and that stop() clears the interval timer.

findings.title: No JSDoc on ShortcutManager class and public methods
findings.description: The ShortcutManager class and its 3 public members (registerAll, unregisterAll, isRegistered) plus the DEFAULT_BINDINGS constant all lack JSDoc. The behavior that registerAll sets registered=true even when some individual shortcuts fail is a significant design decision that should be documented.
findings.suggestion: Add JSDoc to the class, each public method, and DEFAULT_BINDINGS. Document that registerAll is idempotent, that partial registration failures are reported via onError but do not prevent the 'registered' flag from being set, and that DEFAULT_BINDINGS uses Electron-style accelerator syntax.

findings.title: ShortcutManagerOptions.unregister is accepted but never consumed
findings.code_snippet: unregister: (accelerator: string) => void
findings.description: Encapsulation: 7/10, Expression: 5/10, Usefulness: 3/10, Enforcement: 5/10 (Aggregate: 5.0). ShortcutManagerOptions requires callers to provide an 'unregister' function, but ShortcutManager's constructor (global-shortcuts.ts:18-24) never stores or uses it. Only 'unregisterAll' is stored. This is a type contract violation: the interface promises the manager will use per-shortcut unregistration, but it only performs bulk unregister. Callers are forced to implement a function that is dead code.
findings.suggestion: Either remove 'unregister' from ShortcutManagerOptions and only keep 'unregisterAll', or store and use it in ShortcutManager (e.g., for individual shortcut removal). If individual removal is planned for the future, add a comment and make it optional: unregister?: (accelerator: string) => void.

findings.title: ClipboardMonitor accepts zero or negative pollIntervalMs without validation
findings.description: Encapsulation: 8/10, Expression: 5/10, Usefulness: 5/10, Enforcement: 3/10 (Aggregate: 5.25). ClipboardMonitorOptions.pollIntervalMs is typed as optional number with a sensible default (1000ms). However, there is no validation that the provided value is positive. A caller passing pollIntervalMs: 0 or pollIntervalMs: -100 would create a setInterval with undefined behavior (Node.js clamps to 1ms, but the intent is clearly wrong). Constructor does not establish the invariant that pollIntervalMs > 0.
findings.suggestion: Add a guard in the constructor: if (this.pollIntervalMs <= 0) throw new Error('pollIntervalMs must be positive'). Alternatively, use Math.max(1, ...) as a floor. For stronger type-level enforcement, consider a branded PositiveNumber type, though that may be excessive for this context.

findings.title: ClipboardChange and ActiveWindowInfo are mutable event/result interfaces
findings.description: Encapsulation: 5/10, Expression: 5/10, Usefulness: 5/10, Enforcement: 5/10 (Aggregate: 5.0). ActiveWindowInfo represents a snapshot of the active window at a point in time -- it should be immutable once created. ClipboardChange is an event emitted by ClipboardMonitor -- events should be immutable as they may be consumed by multiple handlers. Neither interface uses readonly modifiers.
findings.suggestion: Add readonly to all fields on both interfaces: export interface ActiveWindowInfo { readonly appName: string; readonly windowTitle: string; readonly pid: number } and similarly for ClipboardChange. This prevents accidental mutation by consumers without any runtime cost.

findings.title: registerAll() has 3-level nesting; extract single-binding registration
findings.description: registerAll() reaches nesting depth 3 (method body > for-loop > try/catch > if-branch). The loop body contains a try/catch with error wrapping and a success check, making the for-loop body 15 lines. Extracting the per-binding registration into a private method reduces nesting by one level and makes the loop body a single call.

findings.title: No integration test for ClipboardMonitor with real system clipboard
findings.code_snippet: readClipboard: () => clipboardText,
findings.description: ClipboardMonitor has thorough unit tests using injected readClipboard callbacks, which is appropriate for the Functional Core. However, unlike active-window which has active-window.integration.test.ts proving the real osascript round-trip works, there is no integration test proving ClipboardMonitor works with the real system clipboard API (e.g., Electron's clipboard.readText()). The unit tests verify the polling/diffing logic but not that the real clipboard binding works end-to-end.
findings.suggestion: Add a clipboard-monitor.integration.test.ts that constructs ClipboardMonitor with electron.clipboard.readText (or a node clipboard library) as the readClipboard callback, writes to the clipboard, and asserts onChange fires. Use describe.runIf() to gate on desktop environment availability.

findings.title: No integration test for ShortcutManager with real Electron globalShortcut
findings.description: ShortcutManager unit tests use injected register/unregister callbacks. While this correctly tests the Functional Core, there is no integration test proving the wiring works with Electron's real globalShortcut.register/unregisterAll APIs.
findings.suggestion: Add a global-shortcuts.integration.test.ts that constructs ShortcutManager with Electron's globalShortcut API (or a conditional describe.runIf that detects Electron environment), registers a shortcut, and verifies isRegistered.

findings.title: ShortcutManagerOptions.unregister is accepted but never used or tested
findings.description: ShortcutManagerOptions requires an 'unregister' function (types.ts line 30), and all tests dutifully pass 'unregister: () => {}'. However, ShortcutManager's constructor never stores options.unregister and the class has no method that calls it. This is a dead parameter that increases the API surface for no reason.
findings.suggestion: Either implement per-accelerator unregister support in ShortcutManager and test it, or remove 'unregister' from ShortcutManagerOptions to avoid misleading callers.

findings.title: No comment documenting macOS-only scope of active-window
findings.code_snippet:
const APPLESCRIPT = `
tell application "System Events"
  set frontApp to first application process whose frontmost is true
  ...
end tell
`
findings.description: The active-window module is hardcoded to macOS via AppleScript with no comment or documentation indicating the platform constraint. In a walking skeleton iteration this is expected, but the scope limitation should be explicitly annotated so future maintainers know this is intentional and not an oversight.
findings.suggestion: Add a file-level or constant-level comment such as: /** Platform: macOS only. Uses osascript/AppleScript. Windows and Linux support requires platform-specific implementations. */

findings.title: Redundant 'as ShortcutAction' casts on DEFAULT_BINDINGS literals
findings.code_snippet:
export const DEFAULT_BINDINGS = [
  { accelerator: 'CommandOrControl+Shift+A', action: 'toggle-panel' as ShortcutAction },
  ...
] as const
findings.description: Each action value is a string literal that already belongs to the ShortcutAction union type. With 'as const' the array is a readonly tuple of literal types, so the 'as ShortcutAction' casts add no type safety and clutter each line. Using 'satisfies' on the whole array would achieve type-checking without redundant per-element casts.
findings.suggestion: Replace per-element 'as ShortcutAction' casts with a single 'as const satisfies readonly ShortcutBinding[]' on the array. Cleaner and catches typos just as well.

findings.title: ClipboardChange.timestamp and pollIntervalMs use bare number for ms values
findings.description: Encapsulation: 7/10, Expression: 5/10, Usefulness: 5/10, Enforcement: 5/10 (Aggregate: 5.5). ClipboardChange.timestamp (line 11) and ClipboardMonitorOptions.pollIntervalMs (line 15) both use bare 'number' for millisecond values. While pollIntervalMs has a clear 'Ms' suffix convention, timestamp does not indicate its unit.
findings.suggestion: At minimum, add JSDoc: /** Unix epoch milliseconds */ timestamp: number. For stronger enforcement, consider a shared branded type: type EpochMs = number & { readonly __brand: 'EpochMs' }.

positive_observations: Clean Functional Core / Imperative Shell separation: ClipboardMonitor and ShortcutManager accept IO callbacks via constructor options, keeping core logic pure and testable without mocks
Excellent test coverage with thorough edge cases: empty input, unicode, double-start idempotency, error propagation with and without onError handlers
Proper error wrapping with cause chains: new Error('...', { cause: err }) provides debuggable error context throughout
getActiveWindow uses execFile with timeout (5000ms) rather than exec, avoiding shell injection and preventing hangs
Test for ClipboardMonitor uses vi.useFakeTimers for deterministic timer testing -- clean and reliable approach
Integration test for active-window guards with describe.runIf(platform() === 'darwin') and includes a sensible osascript availability check
No forbidden patterns (TODO/FIXME/HACK/PLACEHOLDER/TEMP) found in any source files
Error messages in code are descriptive and include context (e.g., 'Failed to register shortcut: ${binding.accelerator}')
The code is clean and self-documenting at the implementation level -- variable names, method names, and class structure are clear
getActiveWindow() wraps execFile errors with new Error(msg, { cause }) -- good error wrapping pattern
ShortcutManager error messages include the accelerator key that failed -- good debuggability context
Number.parseInt with Number.isNaN guard in parseAppleScriptOutput -- defensive parsing
ClipboardMonitor and ShortcutManager both use the onError optional callback pattern consistently with the rest of the codebase
All functions are short (<30 lines), consistent with project conventions
Named constant DEFAULT_POLL_INTERVAL_MS used instead of magic number in clipboard-monitor
Clean guard clauses with early returns throughout (start/stop/registerAll/unregisterAll)
Classes reserved for Imperative Shell while pure logic (parseAppleScriptOutput) is a plain function -- matches FC/IS architecture
Types cleanly separated into dedicated types.ts file
parseAppleScriptOutput tests are exemplary Functional Core unit tests: no mocks, direct input/output, comprehensive edge cases
active-window.integration.test.ts demonstrates correct pattern for OS-level integration testing with platform gating
Timer-based tests correctly use vi.useFakeTimers() which is appropriate infrastructure for deterministic timer testing
ShortcutAction is a proper 4-value literal union -- consistent with codebase best practices for domain enumerations
Dependency injection via options interfaces keeps Imperative Shell concerns testable without requiring mocks of system APIs
